elements.sent-2411-001: Utilising classical results on the structure of Hopf algebras, we develop a novel approach for the construction of cubature formulae on Wiener space based on unshuffle expansions.
elements.sent-2411-002: We demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach by constructing the first explicit degree-7 cubature formula on $d$-dimensional Wiener space with drift, in the sense of Lyons and Victoir \cite{LV04 .}
elements.sent-2411-003: The support of our degree-7 formula is significantly smaller than that of currently implemented or proposed constructions.
elements.sent-2411-004: Cubature, in combination with Taylor expansion for error estimation, is a classical and efficient method for approximating the integrals of sufficiently regular functions of several variables.
elements.sent-2411-005: The cubature paradigm replaces a target measure with a discrete measure of small, finite support that exactly matches the integrals of a finite-dimensional space of (polynomial) test functions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Cubature on Wiener space (as developed by Lyons and Victoir \cite {LV04 ) extends this concept to path-space: the traditional Taylor expansion is replaced by the stochastic Taylor expansion, and Stratonovich iterated integrals of Brownian motion take the place of polynomials.}
elements.sent-2411-007: In its iterated form known as the Kusuoka-Lyons-Victoir (KLV) method (see Lyons-Victoir \cite{LV04 and Kusuoka \cite{kusuoka ), cubature on Wiener space provides high-order weak approximations of measures evolving in infinite-dimensional spaces.}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The KLV method, in its basic form, approximates a broad class of potentially hypo-elliptic parabolic partial differential equations (PDEs) by computing weak solutions of stochastic differential equations (SDEs).
elements.sent-2411-009: Unlike classical cubature methods, it can be shown using Malliavin calculus that these approximations achieve high-order accuracy even when the test function only has Lipschitz regularity.
elements.sent-2411-010: This foundational work has opened up a range of cubature applications, including McKean-Vlasov SDEs \cite{McKeanVlasov , \cite{CMM , backward stochastic differential equations \cite{CM1 , \cite{CM2 , sensitivity analysis for financial derivatives \cite{T , stochastic partial differential equations \cite{BT , and the non-linear filtering problem \cite{CG , \cite{CO .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Numerical implementations of cubature on Wiener space combining the KLV\ method with partial sampling techniques in Gyurko, Lyons \cite{gyurko and recombination in Ninomiya, Shinozaki \cite{ninomiya demonstrate the high-order convergence properties of cubature up to degree nine.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: These implementations also show that high-degree cubature approximations can be significantly more efficient than first- and second-order methods, especially when high-accuracy solutions are required.
elements.sent-2411-013: A numerical toy example implemented in \cite{LL12 used high-degree Gaussian quadrature to make the recombining KLV method adaptive to the regularity of the boundary data.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Although this approach produces exceptionally accurate and efficient approximations of the PDE solution, the high-degree cubature measures on path-space necessary for extending this methodology to practical problems remain unavailable.
elements.sent-2411-015: The construction of efficient, high-degree cubature measures for more than two- or three-dimensional noise remains a significant challenge for Wiener space.
elements.sent-2411-016: The algebraic and computational complexity increases rapidly with the degree of the approximation, see e.g.\ \cite{gyurko .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Explicit constructions of cubature measures on Wiener space broadly involve three steps.
elements.sent-2411-018: The expected signature of Brownian motion (with drift) is expanded in a convenient basis.
elements.sent-2411-019: This leads to the formulation of a moment problem determined by a system polynomial equations that needs to be solved by a random variable taking values in the free Lie algebra.
elements.sent-2411-020: Finally, the most challenging step involves realising this random variable as a polynomials of (a small family of) distributions for which (possibly efficient) finite-dimensional joint cubature measures of the necessary orders are available.
elements.sent-2411-021: Replacing these random variables with their cubature measures yields the cubature measure on Wiener space.
elements.sent-2411-022: In this paper we propose a novel approach based on unshuffle expansions that leverages the Hopf algebra structure of the tensor algebra.
elements.sent-2411-023: Instead of a basis, we use a redundant spanning set induced by the linear extension of the logarithm restricted to grouplike elements, in order to expand the expected signature.
elements.sent-2411-024: This map is also known as the canonical projection onto the free Lie algebra \cite{Reu03 , or Eulerian idempotent \cite{Lod94,Pat93 , and occurs naturally in the non-commutative correction terms of the Campbell-Baker-Hausdorff formula.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: The choice to abandon the use of a basis, while counterintuitive, has the benefit of leveraging the symmetries of the Wiener measure in a way that makes the construction more tractable.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our approach has several advantages over existing methods in \cite{shinozaki,hayakawa-tanaka,litterer, gyurko .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The unshuffle expansion of the expected signature in this spanning set leads to a much simplified and sparser moment problem.
elements.sent-2411-028: This greatly mitigates the technical complexity that makes existing approaches increasingly intractable for higher degrees As consequence we can construct a degree-seven cubature measure on Wiener space that in contrast to existing constructions can be stated explicitly for arbitrary dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-029: We also how to solve the resulting moment problem efficiently by constructing discrete random variables by introducing lower-degree, independent, auxiliary Gaussian cubatures.
elements.sent-2411-030: This results in measures with vastly smaller and, hence, more efficient supports than existing constructions.
elements.sent-2411-031: Constructions for cubature measures on Wiener space of degree three and five were first obtained in Lyons, Victoir \cite{LV04 by solving moment problems for discrete random variables arising from the expansion of the non-commutative exponential in a Poincare-Birkhoff-Witt basis of the free tensor algebra.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Since then several attempts have been made to develop constructions of cubature measures on Wiener space that extend to higher degrees.
elements.sent-2411-033: For the one- and two-dimensional noise Litterer \cite{litterer}\ generalises the Lyons-Victoir construction to degree seven.
elements.sent-2411-034: Using a similar approach Gyurko and Lyons \cite{gyurko obtained measures of degree nine and eleven for one-dimensional noise.}
elements.sent-2411-035: While these constructions result in efficient, explicit cubature formulae on Wiener space with the smallest support known to date they do not extend in any obvious and tractable way to higher dimensions (cf.\ Section \ref{sec:3D where we have extend the Lyons-Victoir construction to the three-dimensional case).}
elements.sent-2411-036: The first construction of a general if not explicit degree-seven cubature measure on Wiener space is due to Shinozaki \cite[Theorem 3.1 and 3.4]{shinozaki , who leverages the algebraic relations of products of iterated stochastic integrals to construct a moment similar family on grouplike elements that matches the expectations of Stratonovich iterated integrals of Brownian motion up to degree seven.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The construction is a remarkable technical achievement but does not lead to a cubature measure on Lie polynomials or paths that can be explicitly stated in dimensions greater than two.
elements.sent-2411-038: Theorem 3.4 is stated for the two-dimensional case and the intrinsic technical complexity of the calculations means it cannot be stated in higher dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-039: Despite this, higher-dimensional examples can at least in principle be obtained from Theorem 3.1 using computer algebra programs \cite[Remark 3.7]{shinozaki .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Shinozaki's construction relies on high-dimensional Gaussian cubature measures.
elements.sent-2411-041: The $7$-moment similar families for $d$-dimensional Brownian motion requires discrete random variables matching sufficient moments of a \begin{equation}2d+3 {d \choose 2} +3{ d \choose 3} =\frac{d^{3}+3d }{2}, \quad d\geq 3\end{equation} dimensional standard normal random variable.
elements.sent-2411-042: For the case of two-dimensional Brownian motion, Shinozaki's construction has been implemented using a seven-dimensional degree-seven Gaussian cubature (Ninomiya, Shinozaki \cite{ninomiya ).}
elements.sent-2411-043: An alternative, randomised construction of cubature measures based on recombination was first proposed in Litterer, Lyons \cite[p.1307-1308]{LL12 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: The algorithm is based on an observation by Wendel \cite{wendel who observed that for a spherically symmetric measure an IID sample $2n$ point contains with probability $1/2$ the origin inside the convex hull of the sample.}
elements.sent-2411-045: In Litterer \cite{litterer the signature was also used as a test function for recombination on path-space.}
elements.sent-2411-046: A sophisticated extension of these ideas in Hayakawa, Tanaka \cite{hayakawa-tanaka and Hayakawa, Oberhauser, Lyons \cite{hayakawa-lyons provides far more general estimates that also apply to the number of paths required for the randomised construction of cubature measures on Wiener space.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Since the randomised construction makes no use of the symmetries inherent in the Wiener measure, the support of the resulting formulae grows with the dimension of the truncated tensor algebra over $d$ variables.
elements.sent-2411-048: The computational complexity in the examples considered grows even faster, making the proposed construction for degree-seven and above numerically intractable even for moderate-dimensional Brownian noise.
elements.sent-2411-049: For degree seven only a two-dimensional example is constructed in \cite{hayakawa-tanaka .}
elements.sent-2411-050: The approach proposed in this paper resolves some significant limitations of existing constructions.
elements.sent-2411-051: It allows us to construct explicit cubature measures for $d$-dimensional Brownian motion based on a $d$-dimensional degree-$7$ Gaussian cubature measures and some auxiliary variables that only have to match Gaussian moments up to degree three.
elements.sent-2411-052: This results in cubature measures on Lie polynomials that are explicit for arbitrary dimension and have in many important cases vastly smaller support compared to any existing construction (cf Section \ref{sec:effic for a detailed discussion).}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section \ref{sec:3D we have, in addition, constructed a new degree-seven cubature measure for three-dimensional Brownian motion that generalises the PBW-basis construction of Lyons and Victoir \cite{LV04 .}}
elements.sent-2411-054: This generalisation provides an important reference point as it allows us to compare the size of its support with that of the measure derived from the general construction based on unshuffle expansions.
elements.sent-2411-055: The support of any cubature measure on Wiener space is trivially bounded below by the size of the support of its underlying $d$-dimensional Gaussian cubature measure (cf.\ Lyons, Victoir \cite {LV04 ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: We can show that the size of the support of our cubature measure for $d$-dimensional noise bounded above by $4d^2$ times the size of the support of any $d$-dimensional degree-seven Gaussian cubature measure (cf.\ Section \ref{sec:gaussiancubatures for a discussion of such measures).}
elements.section-2411-002: The tensor Hopf algebra and its Eulerian idempotent
elements.sent-2411-057: In this section we provide the algebra background which will be used for the construction of the cubature formula; for details we refer to \cite{Reu03 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional vector space.
elements.sent-2411-059: We denote the tensor algebra \begin{equation}T(V) \coloneqq \bigoplus_{n = 0}^\infty V^{\otimes n}\end{equation} endowed with the tensor product $\otimes$, making $T(V)$ the free (non-commutative) algebra generated by $V$.
elements.sent-2411-060: As a vector space, it is spanned by elementary tensors $\a_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes \a_n$ with $\a_k \in V$, which we identify with words $\a_1\ldots \a_n$, omitting the tensor product symbol; we use $1$ to denote the empty word, the generator of $\mathbb R = V^{\otimes 0}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: The unshuffle coproduct is defined by \begin{equation}\Delta_\shuffle \colon T(V) \to T(V) \otimes T(V) \a_1\ldots \a_n \mapsto \sum_{k = 0}^n\sum_{\sigma \in Sh(k,n-k)} \a_{\sigma(1)}\ldots \a_{\sigma(k)} \otimes \a_{\sigma(k+1)}\ldots \a_{\sigma(n)}\end{equation} and $\Delta_\shuffle 1 \coloneqq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Here $\mathrm{Sh (p,q)$ denotes the set of $(p,q)$-shuffles, i.e.\ permutations in $\mathfrak{S _{p+q}$ which preserve the order of $1,\ldots,p$ and $p+1,\ldots,p+q$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: An intuitive way of thinking of $\Delta_\shuffle$ is that it separates a word $\a_1\ldots \a_n$ into two subwords (elements of $T(V)$ of the form $\a_{k_1 \ldots \a_{k_m}$ with $k_1 < \ldots < k_m$) without altering the order of each subword.. For example, \begin{equation}\label{ex:abc} \Delta_\shuffle (\a\b\c) = 1 \otimes \a \b \c + \a \otimes \b \c + \b \otimes \a \c + \c \otimes \a \b + \b \c \otimes \a + \a \c \otimes \b + \a \b \otimes \c + \a \b \c \otimes 1 \,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The coproduct is coassociative, i.e.\ its iterates are well-defined independently of the order: define \begin{equation}\Delta_\shuffle^1 \coloneqq \mathbbm 1_{T(V)}, \quad \Delta^n_\shuffle \coloneqq (\mathbbm 1 \otimes \Delta^{n-1}_\shuffle) \otimes \Delta_\shuffle = ( \Delta^{n-1}_\shuffle \otimes \mathbbm 1) \otimes \Delta_\shuffle \colon T(V) \to T(V)^{\otimes n}\end{equation} for $n \geq 2$ (so that in particular $\Delta_\shuffle = \Delta_\shuffle^2$).
elements.sent-2411-065: It can be shown that $\Delta_\shuffle$ is also an algebra morphism, and that $\otimes$ is a coalgebra product, making $(T(V), \otimes, \Delta_\shuffle)$ a bialgebra; since it is graded and connected, it can be endowed with a (unique) antipode, making it a Hopf algebra.
elements.sent-2411-066: Also note that $\Delta_\shuffle$ is cocommutative, i.e.\ $\tau \circ \Delta_\shuffle = \Delta_\shuffle$ where $\tau$ is flips the two copies of $T(V)$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Note that, by cocommutativity, it is possible to express $\Delta_\shuffle^n$ by summing over ordered submultisets and using symmetric products, instead of subwords (for example, in \eqref{ex:abc , $\beta \otimes \alpha\gamma$ and $\alpha\gamma \otimes \beta$ can be grouped together); this is advantageous in implementations, as it reduces the number of terms factorially.}
elements.sent-2411-068: We will define the (iterated) reduced coproduct by \begin{equation}\widetilde \Delta_\shuffle^n \coloneqq \pi_{\geq 1}^{\otimes n} \circ \Delta_\shuffle^n, \qquad where  \pi_{\geq 1} \colon T(V) \twoheadrightarrow \bigoplus_{n = 1}^\infty V^{\otimes n}\end{equation} is the projection onto tensors of positive degree (for example $\widetilde \Delta_\shuffle x = \Delta_\shuffle x - x \otimes 1 - 1 \otimes x$).
elements.sent-2411-069: We introduce similar notation for the projections of the tensor algebra onto its graded components \begin{equation}\pi_n \colon T(V) \twoheadrightarrow \bigoplus_{k = 0}^n V^{\otimes k}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: It will be helpful to use sum-free Sweedler notation \begin{equation}\Delta_\shuffle^n w \eqqcolon w_{(1)} \otimes \cdots \otimes w_{(n)}, \quad \widetilde\Delta_\shuffle^n w \eqqcolon w^{(1)} \otimes \cdots \otimes w^{(n)} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\Delta_\shuffle^n w \eqqcolon w_{(1)} \otimes \cdots \otimes w_{(n)}, \quad \widetilde\Delta_\shuffle^n w \eqqcolon w^{(1)} \otimes \cdots \otimes w^{(n)} \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-071: Recall the symmetric tensor product, defined for $x_1,\ldots,x_n \in T(V)$: \begin{equation}(x_1 , \ldots , x_n ) \coloneqq \frac{1}{n!} \sum_{\sigma \in \mathfrak S_n} x_{\sigma(1)} \otimes \cdots \otimes x_{\sigma(n)}\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-072: Note that symmetric product is not associative, e.g.\ the above product cannot be computed recursively by $((x_1 , \ldots , x_{n-1 ) , x_n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The convolution product on linear endomorphisms of the vector space $T(V)$ is defined by \begin{equation}\star \colon End(T(V))^{\otimes 2} \to End(T(V)), \qquad f \star g \coloneqq \otimes \circ (f \otimes g) \circ \Delta_\shuffle\end{equation} Here \say{$\otimes \circ$} just denotes the product mapping $T(V)^{\otimes 2 \to T(V)$, and note that by cocommutativity this can be replaced by the symmetric tensor product.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Thanks to the bialgebra properties, multiple convolution products can be written using the iterated coproduct: \begin{equation}\label{eq:star} f_1 \star \cdots \star f_n = \otimes^n \circ (f_1 \otimes \cdots \otimes f_n) \circ \Delta_\shuffle^n \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: For $f \in \mathrm{End (V)$, consider its exponential and logarithmic series under the convolution product: \begin{equation}\exp_\star(f) \coloneqq \sum_{n \geq 0} \frac{f^{\star n}}{n!}, \qquad \log_\star (f) \coloneqq \sum_{n \geq 0} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n}(f - \pi_0)^{\star n}\end{equation} Note that when applied to any given element, these series always reduce to finite sums by conilpotency (the property that for any $x \in T(V)$ there exists $n$ such that $\Delta^n_\shuffle x = 0$).}
elements.sent-2411-076: The Eulerian idempotent is denoted $\pi_1$ in \cite[\S 3.2]{Reu03 (not to be confused with the map $\pi_1$ as it is denoted here, the projection onto single letters); we also refer to \cite{Pat93, Lod94 for further details.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Recall that we can define a Lie bracked on $T(V)$ by $[x,y] \coloneqq x \otimes y - y \otimes x$.
elements.sent-2411-078: The free Lie algebra $\mathcal L(V)$ over $V$ is the smallest Lie subalgebra of $T(V)$ which contains $V$; it can be explicitly described as the direct sum \begin{equation}\mathcal L(V) = V \oplus [V,V] \oplus [[V,V],V] \oplus \ldots\end{equation} where Lie brackets between vector subspaces of $T(V)$ denote the space spanned by all Lie brackets of the two vector spaces; it can also be described more abstractly by a universal property, without reference to $T(V)$.
elements.sent-2411-079: It is a non-trivial result that $\mathcal L(V)$ coincides with the space of $\Delta_\shuffle$-primitive elements: \begin{equation}\mathcal L(V) = \{x \in T(V) \mid \widetilde \Delta_\shuffle x = 0\} \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-080: The expression for the Eulerian idempotent can be made more explicit by \begin{equation}\e(x) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} (\mathbbm 1 - \pi_0)^{\star n}(x) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} \otimes^n \circ (\mathbbm 1 - \pi_0)^{\otimes n} \circ \Delta_\shuffle^n(x) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} ( x^{(1)} , \ldots , x^{(n)}) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-081: In the first identity we have used the bialgebra property to express convolution powers, and in the second we have used cocommutativity to symmetrise the tensor product (and note that the Sweedler notation is reduced).
elements.sent-2411-082: We mention for the sake of completeness, though it will not be used in the following pages, that $x \in T(\!(V)\!) \coloneqq \prod_{n = 0 ^\infty V^{\otimes n}$ is grouplike if $\Delta_\shuffle x = x \otimes x$, and denote the group of these $\mathcal G(\!(V)\!)$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Calling $\mathcal G^n(V) \coloneqq \pi_n(\mathcal G(\!(V)\!))$, it still holds that for $x \in \mathcal G^n(V)$, $\Delta_\shuffle x = x \otimes x$ on $T^n(V)$.
elements.sent-2411-084: Therefore, calling $\mathcal G(V) \coloneqq \bigcup_{n \geq 0 \mathcal G^n(V) \subset T(V)$, we have that for $x \in \mathcal G(V)$ \begin{equation}\e(x) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} \otimes^n \circ (\mathbbm 1 - 1)^{\otimes n} \circ \Delta_\shuffle^n(x) = \sum_{n \geq 1} \frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{n} x^{\otimes n} = \log (x)\,,\end{equation} i.e.\ $\e|_{\mathcal G(V) = \log|_{\mathcal G(V)}$, where the latter denotes the logarithmic series taken w.r.t.\ the tensor product.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In fact, $\e$ is the unique linear map $T(V) \to T(V)$ with this property, where uniqueness follows from the fact that $\mathcal G(V)$ spans $T(V)$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Recall that the inverse of $\log$ is the tensor exponential $\exp$, whose expression on a finite sum can be written in terms of the symmetric product as follows, for $a_1,\ldots,a_n \in T(V)$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:exp} \begin{split} \exp \Big( \sum_{k = 1}^n a_k \Big) = \sum_{\substack{m \geq 0 k_1,\ldots, k_m = 1,\ldots,n}} \frac{1}{m!} (a_{k_1}, \ldots, a_{k_m}) = \sum_{\substack{m \geq 0 m_1 + \ldots + m_n = m}} \frac{(\overbrace{a_1,\ldots, a_1}^{m_1}, \ldots, \overbrace{a_n,\ldots,a_n}^{m_n})}{m_1! \cdots m_n!} \end{split}\end{equation} We summarise the properties of $\e$ of relevance to us in the following proposition.

elements.section-2411-003: Cubature measures for Wiener space supported on Lie polynomials
elements.sent-2411-106: Let $S(\circ \widehat B)$ denote the Stratonovich signature of Brownian motion augmented with time in its zero-th coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-107: We recall the following definition of Lyons and Victoir.
elements.sent-2411-108: By the scaling property of Brownian motion, a cubature measure on the interval $[0,T]$ can be obtained from $Q$ above by letting $\omega_{T,i ^{j (t)=\sqrt{T}\omega _{i ^{j (t/T)$, $j=1,\ldots ,d$ and keeping the weights of $Q$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The drift component of the cubature paths is given by $\omega^0(t) = t$.
elements.sent-2411-110: In the KLV method, cubature measures are used to weakly approximate solutions to $\mathbb R^e$-valued Stratonovich stochastic differential equation (SDE) of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:SDE} \dif X_{t}=F(X_t) \circ \dif \widehat B_{t},\end{equation} with sufficiently regular vector fields $F$ which contains drift $F_0$ and the diffusion coefficients $F_k$, $k = 1,\ldots, d$.
elements.sent-2411-111: The KLV approximation is computed by solving differential equations controlled by the cubature paths $ \omega _{j}$ \begin{equation}\dif X_{t}=F(X_t) \dif \omega_j(t) .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: It is well-known that the one-step order-$m$ Euler expansion of such a controlled differential equation on the interval $[s,t]$ is equal to, up to an error of order $(t-s)^{(m+1)/2}$ to the ODE \begin{equation}\label{eq:log-ODE} \dot Z_t = F^\circ(Z) (\widetilde \pi_m(\log S_{s,t}(\omega_j))), \quad Z_s = X_s\end{equation} where the map $F^\circ:\mathcal L(\mathbb R^{1+d ) \to C^\infty(\mathbb R^e, \mathbb R^e)$ is the universal extension of the linear map that takes basis elements $\epsilon_i \in \mathbb R^{1+d}$ to vector fields $F_i$, see e.g.\ Kusuoka \cite{kusuoka .}}

elements.section-2411-004: Extension of the Lyons-Victoir construction to degree-\texorpdfstring{$7$}
elements.sent-2411-121: In this section we briefly outline the construction of a novel degree-seven cubature for three-dimensional noise directly generalising the Lyons, Victoir construction \cite{LV04 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: While this construction is specific to three dimensions it provides a valuable reference point for the relative size of the support (and, hence, efficiency) of the cubature measures later obtained using unshuffling expansions (cf.\ Remark \ref{benchmark remark ).}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Expansion of the expected signature over a symmetrised spanning set
elements.sent-2411-161: The development of a cubature formula first requires the expression of the expected signature projected into the Lie algebra.
elements.sent-2411-162: This subsection contains all of the details for computing this expansion using Eulerian idempotent elements.
elements.sent-2411-163: This method is used as an alternative to selecting a specific Hall basis for the free Lie algebra and we will discuss the relative tractability between both methods.
elements.sent-2411-164: The subsection will conclude with an explicit expression for the expected signature truncated at degree 7, an expression which is far more succinct than any other currently proposed.
elements.sent-2411-165: To begin, recall that the expected signature of a Brownian motion, $B$, in $\mathbb{R ^d$ is given by \begin{equation}\label{equation:sig_bm} \mathbb{E}[S_{0,1}(\circ B)] =\exp \bigg (\frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^d\epsilon_i^{\otimes 2}\bigg ) = 1 + \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{1}{2^nn!}\sum_{1\leq i_1,\dots,i_n \leq d}\epsilon_{i_1}^{\otimes 2}\dots\epsilon_{i_n}^{\otimes 2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-166: It is often useful to augment the Brownian motion with a zeroth time coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-168: The expected signature of $\widehat{B}$ can be retrieved from that of $B$ with the addition of a number of terms.
elements.sent-2411-169: At degree 7, \begin{equation}\label{equation:sig_bm_drift} \begin{split} \mathbb{E}[\widetilde\pi_7 (S_{0,1}(\circ \widehat{B}))] ={} \mathbb{E}[\widetilde\pi_7(S_{0,1}(\circ B))] + \epsilon_0 + \frac 12\epsilon_0^2 + \frac 16\epsilon_0^3+\sum_{1\leq i\leq d}\Big (\frac 12(\epsilon_0,\epsilon_i^2) + \frac 14(\epsilon_0,\epsilon_0,\epsilon_i^2)\Big ) + \sum_{1\leq i,j\leq d}\frac 14(\epsilon_0,\epsilon_i^2,\epsilon_j^2). \end{split}\end{equation} We focus first on the non-augmented case (or ``drift-free Brownian motion'') and choose $\delta_n$ to be proportional to the $2n^\text{th}$ level by defining, \begin{equation}\delta_n \coloneqq \sum_{1\leq i_1,\dots,i_n \leq d}\epsilon_{i_1}^{\otimes 2}\dots\epsilon_{i_n}^{\otimes 2}.\end{equation}.
